myth: O(n log n) is always faster than O(n²)
truth: Big-O describes asymptotic behavior. For small n, O(n²) algorithms like insertion sort can be faster due to lower constant factors and cache friendliness. That's why Timsort switches to insertion sort for small runs (<64 elements).

Average case is O(1) but worst case is O(n) with hash collisions. Choosing a good hash function, load factor management, and collision resolution strategy are critical. Java 8+ converts HashMap chains to trees at 8 entries.

myth: Recursion is always slower than iteration
truth: Tail-recursive functions can be optimized to iteration by the compiler (tail call optimization). The overhead of recursion is the function call stack. Some problems (tree traversal, divide-and-conquer) are naturally recursive and cleaner to express that way.

myth: BFS always uses more memory than DFS
truth: BFS uses O(branching_factor^depth) memory. DFS uses O(depth) stack memory. But for wide, shallow graphs BFS may use less memory than DFS on deep graphs. The real comparison depends on graph structure.

myth: Greedy algorithms always give optimal solutions
truth: Greedy works only when the problem has the greedy-choice property AND optimal substructure. Fractional knapsack: greedy works. 0/1 knapsack: greedy fails, need DP. Always prove greedy correctness via exchange argument.

myth: Linked lists are better than arrays for insertion
truth: Linked list insertion at a known position is O(1), but finding that position is O(n). Arrays have better cache locality (spatial locality principle). In practice, arrays/vectors outperform linked lists for most workloads due to CPU cache effects.
